_rotation_note: The daily job picks the next business each morning - rotating among ward businesses with priority to spotlight requests from owners and newly opened spots. Keep yesterday's entries in history.

current.name: Girl & the Goat
current.blurb: Chef Stephanie Izard's flagship put Randolph Street's Restaurant Row on the national map. The wood-fired, globally-spiced small plates, from the famous goat empanadas to the pan-fried shishito peppers, still pack the room nightly.

history.name: Lou Mitchell's
history.blurb: A Chicago breakfast landmark at the original starting line of Route 66, serving since 1923. Lou Mitchell's is famous for double-yolk eggs, fluffy skillet omelettes served in the pan, and the free Milk Duds and donut holes handed out while you wait for a table.

history.name: Green Street Smoked Meats
history.blurb: Tucked down an alley off Green Street, this rollicking barbecue joint serves Texas-style brisket, ribs, and burnt ends by the pound on butcher paper. Cold beer, picnic tables, and a lively bar make it a West Loop staple.

history.name: Monteverde Restaurant & Pastificio
history.blurb: Chef Sarah Grueneberg, a James Beard Award winner, turns out some of the country's most celebrated handmade pasta from an open pastificio counter on Madison Street. The cacio whey pepe and the ragu alla napoletana are neighborhood legends, and the bar pours a deep Italian wine list.

history.name: Mario's Italian Lemonade
history.blurb: A Taylor Street summer institution since 1954, Mario's is the little red-and-green stand where Chicagoans line up for hand-shaved Italian lemonade in flavors from lemon and cantaloupe to peach and watermelon. Cash only, open only in the warm months, and worth every minute of the wait.

history.name: Sepia
history.blurb: Set in a restored 1890s print shop, this Michelin-starred West Loop mainstay pairs refined American cooking with a warm, timeless room. It has been a special-occasion favorite in the neighborhood for well over a decade.

history.name: The Publican
history.blurb: A beer-hall-style anchor of Fulton Market, The Publican built its name on oysters, house charcuterie, and pork served at long communal tables. It helped put the neighborhood on the map as a dining destination.

history.name: The Original Pancake House
history.blurb: A breakfast institution since 1953, famous for the oven-baked Dutch Baby and apple pancakes. The West Loop location on Madison fills its striped-awning patio all summer long.

history.name: Lou Mitchell's
history.blurb: A Chicago breakfast landmark at the original starting line of Route 66, serving since 1923. Lou Mitchell's is famous for double-yolk eggs, skillet omelettes served in the pan, and the free Milk Duds and donut holes handed out while you wait.

history.name: Au Cheval
history.blurb: The dark, clubby diner behind what many call the best cheeseburger in America. The wait on Randolph Street is legendary, the bologna sandwich and the eggs are worth it, and the whole room hums well past midnight.
